sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Scoliosis and postural deformity screening aims to identify who may need observation, exercise-based management, or specialist spinal review rather than trying to solve every asymmetry at first contact.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Visible asymmetry of shoulders, ribs, waist, or trunk
- Prominence on forward bend
- Postural fatigue, back pain, or cosmetic concern
- Adolescent growth phase or long-standing spinal asymmetry history

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Severe pain, neurological signs, systemic symptoms, rapid curve progression, or unusual stiffness are not typical uncomplicated scoliosis features and should escalate review
- Not every posture difference is structural scoliosis
- Screening is about recognition and referral thresholds, not definitive Cobb-angle diagnosis in the field

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen age, growth status, functional impact, and obvious asymmetry
- Escalate if neurological symptoms, severe pain, or rapid progression concern exists
- Reassure that mild asymmetry alone is not automatically dangerous

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Use structured posture and forward-bend observation where appropriate
- Refer for imaging or specialist spinal review when structural scoliosis is likely or progression risk is meaningful
- Use exercise-based support for function and confidence within scope, but avoid claiming to diagnose curve severity without imaging

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The most important questions are whether the curve is structural, progressing, and affecting function or safety
